message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Estimating the transmissibility of infectious diseases is key to inform situational awareness and for response planning. Several methods tend to overestimate the basic (R
                    <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                    ) and effective (R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    ) reproduction numbers during the initial phases of an epidemic. The reasons driving the observed bias are unknown. In this work we explore the impact of incomplete observations and underreporting of the first generations of infections during the initial epidemic phase.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We propose a debiasing procedure which utilises a linear exponential growth model to infer unobserved initial generations of infections and apply it to EpiEstim. We assess the performance of our adjustment using simulated data, considering different levels of transmissibility and reporting rates. We also apply the proposed correction to SARS-CoV-2 incidence data reported in Italy, Sweden, the United Kingdom and the United States of America.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    In all simulation scenarios, our adjustment outperforms the original EpiEstim method. The proposed correction reduces the systematic bias and the quantification of uncertainty is more precise, as better coverage of the true R
                    <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                    values is achieved with tighter credible intervals. When applied to real world data, the proposed adjustment produces basic reproduction number estimates which closely match the estimates obtained in other studies while making use of a minimal amount of data.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The proposed adjustment refines the reproduction number estimates obtained with the current EpiEstim implementation by producing improved, more precise estimates earlier than with the original method. This has relevant public health implications.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Summary</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We propose a back-imputation procedure tackling the issue of unobserved initial generations of infections to reduce the bias observed in the early
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>0</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    estimates and apply it to EpiEstim using simulated and reported COVID-19 data to evaluate it.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Refining reproduction number estimates to account for unobserved generations of infection in emerging epidemics

reference.unstructured: Thompson RN , Hollingsworth TD , Isham V , et al. Key questions for modelling COVID-19 exit strategies: COVID-19 Exit Strategies. Proc R Soc B Biol Sci 2020; 287.
Hethcote HW . The Mathematics of Infectious Diseases. 2000. Available at: http://www.siam.org/journals/ojsa.php.

O’Driscoll M , Harry C , Donnelly CA , Cori A , Dorigatti I. A Comparative Analysis of Statistical Methods to Estimate the Reproduction Number in Emerging Epidemics, With Implications for the Current Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19) Pandemic. Clin Infect Dis 2020; :1–9.

reference.article-title: Estimating epidemic exponential growth rate and basic reproduction number

reference.unstructured: Bettencourt LMA , Ribeiro RM . Real time bayesian estimation of the epidemic potential of emerging infectious diseases. PLoS One 2008; 3.
Dorigatti I , Okell L , Cori A , et al. Report 4: Severity of 2019-novel coronavirus (nCoV). Available at: https://doi.org/10.25561/77154. Accessed 19 April 2021.

reference.article-title: Estimating the generation interval for coronavirus disease (COVID-19) based on symptom onset data, March 2020

reference.unstructured: Arif Billah MI , Mamun Miah M , Nuruzzaman Khan MI . Reproductive number of coronavirus: A systematic review and meta-analysis based on global level evidence. 2020; Available at: https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0242128.

reference.article-title: Estimate of the Basic Reproduction Number for COVID-19: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis

reference.article-title: A comprehensive estimation of country-level basic reproduction numbers R0 for COVID-19: Regime regression can automatically estimate the end of the exponential phase in epidemic data
